6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every AI coding demo ends at the same moment. The feature works, the tests pass, the presenter smiles, cut to pricing page.
Notice what you never see: minute thirty of that codebase’s life. Month six. The new hire trying to understand what the agent built. The bug that lives in four copies of the same pasted logic. The demo ends where software’s actual costs begin, and that’s not an accident, because the numbers from that part of the story are the least flattering in the entire AI coding narrative.
They’re also the best documented. So let’s do the part of the demo nobody performs.
The industry has a dashcam, and it’s recording
The most valuable dataset on this question comes from GitClear, an analytics company that has been classifying code changes since long before the AI wave, across a corpus that has grown past 200 million changed lines of code from real private and open source repositories. Because their data starts in 2020, it captures the before and after of AI assisted development like a dashcam.
Their findings, published across successive annual reports, read like a slow motion incident:
Code duplication exploded. The frequency of duplicated code blocks rose eightfold in…
